items.q: How do waves erode a cliff?
items.correct: Through hydraulic action (water compressing air in cracks), abrasion (waves throwing rocks against cliffs), attrition (rocks smashing against each other), and solution.
items.misconception: Waves erode cliffs by pushing water against the rock until it breaks.
items.random_wrong: Cliffs erode because of acid rain dissolving them.
items.why: Water pressure alone is rarely enough. The main mechanisms involve compressed air and rocks acting as tools — students underestimate how erosion needs material to do the wearing.

items.correct: The zigzag movement of sediment along a coast caused by waves approaching at an angle (swash up the beach) and gravity returning water perpendicular (backwash).
items.misconception: Sand moves along the beach because tides pull it sideways.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift is the rising of sea level over time.
items.why: Students confuse tides with waves. Longshore drift is a wave-driven process — the angle of swash vs straight-down backwash creates the zigzag, not tidal pull.

items.correct: The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries, bounded by a watershed (high ground that separates one basin from another).
items.misconception: A drainage basin is the bowl-shaped area at the mouth of the river.
items.random_wrong: A drainage basin is the underground source of a river.
items.why: Students take 'basin' too literally as a bowl. It is the entire catchment area — often a huge region, not a depression near the river itself.

items.q: Which erosion process involves rocks KNOCKING TOGETHER and becoming smoother?
items.why: Correct answer: Attrition. “Hydraulic action” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What transport process carries DISSOLVED minerals invisibly in water?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Solution. “Traction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many types of EROSION (river/coast) do you need to learn for GCSE?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
